what_it_does: A critical, evidence-tagged inventory of the company's ~66 MCP tools, grouped into functional families, giving each tool a human-facing name and description, its verified real behavior, concrete uses, usage evidence, and defect flags; it also clusters broken or overclaiming tools into five root causes and doubles as the FACE 3 human-meaning layer for tool rendering.
observations: Claims ~66 unique tools after deduplication, split into ~40 real/working, ~16 never-wired/broken/dead, and ~15 overclaiming.
Five root-cause clusters are called out: cwd-relative discover bug, reasoning-model budget gap, mark governance bypass, browser-unreachable chain library, and built-twice channel registries.
Each tool entry follows a fixed template: human name, human description, what it really does, concrete uses, ever-used status, and critical flags.
Every claim is tagged with an evidence law: Verified, Observed, or Inferred, and grounded by file:line, live-test result, or on-disk evidence.
The file explicitly serves as the FACE 3 layer, meaning its human_name and human_description fields are the embedded UI copy the tool-face renders.
It references convergence documents: COMMISSION-COMPLETION-REGISTER.md, TOOL-FACE-BUILD.md, and CHANNEL-LOOP-BOARD.md.
It reconciles a contradiction between two prior passes about whether run_cascade/save_cascade were ever used.
The provided excerpt is truncated; the full catalog and the §MERGE block are not visible.
No code symbols are present in this file.
standouts: Root Cause 3: the mark tool has a verified governance bypass — any caller can write decision_take/decision_retract and flip a decision state without operator attribution.
Root Cause 2: default max_tokens of 256/512 empties reasoning models, so the 2026-06-20 cloud-routing fix is a false green: reachable does not mean productive.
Root Cause 1: a cwd-relative discover bug silently empties dials, operator, and panel; operator's failure is especially dangerous because agents lose Tim's standing rules.
The document merges an internal skeptical audit with the canonical human-facing tool descriptions.
conventions: Markdown headings and bullet lists with family-based grouping.
Fixed per-tool template: human name, human description, really does, concrete uses, ever-used, flags.
Critical issues marked with ★ and root-cause references.
Distinguishes honest-empty results from silent-empty failures.
Notes fail-loud behavior and teaching-loud errors explicitly.
Cross-references other files by name for convergence.
concerns: The excerpt is truncated, so the §MERGE block and later families are not inspectable here.
Many Verified claims rely on evidence held outside this file; a reader must trust the author's grounding without inline file:line or test output.
Mixing audit findings with UI-facing copy creates drift risk if tool behavior changes but the human descriptions are not updated.
Heavy forward references to other documents make isolated validation difficult.
notes: This is the skeptical audit pass that maps which MCP tools actually work, which silently fail, and which overclaim, while also supplying the human-facing tool descriptions for the UI.
questions: What is the exact content of the §MERGE section at the bottom?
Which specific file:line references ground each Verified claim?
How were the 8 tools investigated in two families deduplicated into one entry each?
What precise criteria separate a 'real-and-working' tool from an 'overclaiming but works' tool?
Is this file maintained manually or regenerated from live tool registries?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to be an honest, evidence-grounded map of every MCP tool and the FACE 3 human-meaning layer; the file does both, matching its stated purpose.
apparent_themes: Skeptical default — hunt what is wrong
Human meaning embedded in engineering audit
Governance and safety gaps surfaced explicitly
Transparency about silent and honest empties
intention_signals: The file is trying to stop the system from advertising capabilities it does not actually have, by forcing every tool claim through a live-test filter and packaging the results as both an internal audit and the user-facing tool descriptions.
novelty: It merges a critical defect audit with the primary UI copy layer, so the same document that tells engineers what is broken also provides the pleasant descriptions end users see.
contribution: A single document that supplies verified human-facing tool descriptions, a structured defect taxonomy with root causes, and a per-tool evidence template that a fused system can use to decide which tools to expose or warn about.
summary_for_embedding: An evidence-tagged audit and human-meaning catalog of ~66 company MCP tools, grouped by family, with verified behavior, usage status, critical flags, and five root-cause clusters of broken or overclaiming tools.
intention_for_embedding: To ground tool claims in live evidence, expose silent failures and governance gaps, and simultaneously feed the human-facing tool descriptions for the UI.
